---
title: "Introduction"
author: "Truong Phi"
---

## Overview

The **Positional Burrows-Wheeler Transform (PBWT)**, introduced by [Richard Durbin in 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3998136/), revolutionized how we handle massive genomic datasets. By applying the principles of the Burrows-Wheeler Transform to positional genetic data, it enables ultra-efficient haplotype matching and search operations that were previously computationally prohibitive.

The PBWT provides an efficient way to sort haplotypes at every position along a chromosome, enabling very fast identification of identical-by-descent (IBD) segments and other types of haplotype matching.

### Purposes

*   **Scalability:** Enable efficient analysis of hundreds of thousands to millions of genomes.
*   **Speed:** Perform haplotype matching and sequence search in time independent of the database size.
*   **Compression:** Represent genotype matrices in a format that naturally facilitates both compression and search.

### Content covered

*   **Construction:** How to build prefix and divergence arrays (**Algorithms 1 & 2**).
*   **Internal Matching:** Identifying shared segments and maximal matches within a population (**Algorithms 3 & 4**).
*   **External Querying:** Mapping new samples against a pre-built PBWT database (**Algorithm 5**).



---

::: {#refs}
:::
